context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 3


Problem 3.1 (20 pts)

Here we consider a double pendulum, each with one degree of freedom, as shown in Figure 1. Mass M1 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to a fixed origin. Its x-coordinate is X1 and it makes an angle θ1 with respect to the vertical (y-axis). Mass M2 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to mass M1. Its x-coordinate is X2 and it makes an angle θ2 with respect to the vertical direction.
You may assume that the 'hinges' at the origin and on mass M1 are frictionless. Gravity points downward in the y-direction. You may assume that all oscillations are small, i.e., that θ1 and θ2 are small and terms of order O(θ^2) are negligible.

[Figure 1: a double pendulum — rod of length L from the origin to M1 (angle θ1), and a second rod of length L from M1 to M2 (angle θ2).]

(a) Write down the equations of motion for the two oscillating masses.
(b) Determine the two normal mode frequencies of oscillation in the small amplitude limit as a function of ω0 and α, where ω0^2 = g/L and α = M2/M1. (You don't have to solve the corresponding amplitude ratios.)
(c) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → ∞? Do your results make sense?
(d) What are the two normal mode frequencies if α → 0? Do your results make sense?


Problem 3.2 (20 pts)

Consider two identical LC circuits as shown in Figure 2. The two inductors are brought close together such that their mutual inductance M results in a coupling between the currents flowing in the two circuits.

[Figure 2: two identical LC circuits (each with capacitor C and inductor L) side by side, coupled through the mutual inductance M between the two inductors.]

(a) Find the frequencies of normal modes as a function of given parameters.
(b) What current patterns correspond to these normal modes? Could you use symmetry arguments to discover these modes?


Problem 3.3 (20 pts)

Consider three identical masses constrained to move on a frictionless circle. The masses are connected with identical springs each with spring constant k (see Figure 3). The circle is large such that you can ignore any effects related to the curvature. The circle is horizontal such that gravity can be ignored.

[Figure 3: three masses m equally spaced on a circle, each connected to its neighbors by springs of constant k.]

(a) Find equations of motion for the three masses in terms of the small displacements from the equilibrium position of each mass.
(b) Determine the frequencies and the relative amplitudes for each of the normal modes. Make a simple sketch of the motion of the masses for each of the normal modes. How many different frequencies are there in this system?
(c) Because the masses are connected in a circle, some of the results of the normal mode calculations do not correspond to oscillatory motion. Explain why.


Problem 3.4 (20 pts)

Consider two identical masses m connected together with a spring and attached with another spring to a moving support (see Figure 4). The support is oscillating vertically and its position is given by h(t) = A·cos(ωt). The Hooke constant of the two identical springs is k. Ignore effects of damping.

[Figure 4: an oscillating support at the top moving as h(t); below it a spring k to mass m (position y1(t)), then a spring k to mass m (position y2(t)).]

(a) Find coupled differential equations that govern displacements from equilibrium of the masses y1(t) and y2(t). Express your results in terms of ω0^2 = k/m. Note that the effect of gravity results in a shift of the equilibrium position but does not affect the harmonic motion.
(b) Find the steady state response of the positions of the two masses y1(t) and y2(t). Make a careful sketch of the amplitude as a function of the driving frequency ω for each of the masses.
(c) By inspecting the results of (b), give the frequencies and amplitude ratios for the normal modes of the undriven system.


Problem 3.5 (20 pts)

Two identical beads, each of mass m, are equally spaced along a massless string of length 3a (see Figure 5). Consider the system to be on a frictionless horizontal surface. Initially both ends of the string are fixed (Δ = 0). Assume that the string is under tension T at all times. Beads can execute small amplitude oscillations perpendicular to the string (displacements are exaggerated in the figure).

[Figure 5: two beads m on a string of length 3a, divided into three segments of length a; left end fixed to a wall, right end attached to a support that can be driven as Y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).]

(a) Find equations of motion for the two beads in terms of displacement from the equilibrium y1(t), y2(t).
(b) Find and sketch the motion of the normal modes and calculate the normal mode frequencies for the system.

Assume now that the rightmost attachment point undergoes harmonic oscillation y(t) = Δ·cos(ω_d·t).

(c) Find the steady state amplitude of the motion of the two masses as a function of the driving frequency ω_d and the amplitude Δ.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on Problem 3.1, especially how to even start the equations of motion for the double pendulum in the small-angle limit. I keep mixing up the coordinates and whether I should use forces or energy. Could you help me start, but maybe also remind me what the overall idea is?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point to pause and think. Before diving in, let me ask you: what are the degrees of freedom of this system? In other words, what are the independent coordinates that fully describe the configuration of both masses at any instant?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling lost. I should not scaffold too much. The best Socratic opening is to ask them to identify what they already know — specifically, what method they prefer (Lagrangian/energy vs Newtonian/forces) and what the degrees of freedom are. This orients them without giving anything away. Keeping it bite-sized as per the rules.

exchanges.student: I think it’s just the two angles, \( \theta_1 \) and \( \theta_2 \), but I’m not fully sure because the problem also mentions \(X_1\) and \(X_2\). I’m a little confused about whether those are separate coordinates or just derived from the angles. Also, if that’s right, I still don’t see the big picture of how that turns into the equations.
exchanges.tutor: You're right that θ1 and θ2 are the two independent degrees of freedom. Now think about this: given θ1 and the rod length L, can you write down X1 explicitly? And then X2? If so, what does that tell you about whether X1 and X2 are truly *independent* coordinates?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track about the degrees of freedom but is confused about whether X1, X2 are independent. I should confirm their reasoning about the degrees of freedom briefly and ask them to think about the relationship between X and theta — this is a small but important clarification that unblocks the next step without giving away anything about the equations of motion themselves.

exchanges.student: I guess \(X_1 = L\sin\theta_1\) and \(X_2 = L\sin\theta_1 + L\sin\theta_2\), so that probably means they’re not independent. But I’m still kind of stuck because once I have that, I don’t know whether I’m supposed to do Newton’s laws on each mass, or jump to kinetic and potential energy, or maybe there’s some standard normal-mode setup I’m supposed to recognize. Could you just give me the general form this usually takes for coupled oscillators so I can map this problem onto it?
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got the geometry right. Now, you have two real choices: Newton's laws or the Lagrangian (energy) approach. Which one feels more natural to you? Pick one, and tell me what the very first thing you'd write down is for that method.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived X1 and X2 and correctly concluded they are not independent — that's good progress worth acknowledging briefly. However, they are now asking me to give them the general form for coupled oscillators, which would bypass their own reasoning. I should not provide that. Instead, I should ask them to commit to a method (Newton or Lagrangian) and take the first step themselves. This is a Socratic nudge toward action rather than a scaffolded template.

exchanges.student: Honestly I don’t know, which is why I was hoping for the broad version first. If I had to pick, maybe Lagrangian, but I’m shaky on what counts as \(T\) and \(V\) here and how much of the trig I’m supposed to keep before the small-angle approximation. That didn’t really clear it up for me, so maybe I need a different angle.
exchanges.tutor: Let's start small. Forget T for now. For a single pendulum of length L with mass M at angle θ, what is the potential energy V? Write it down, then we'll build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is hesitating and asking for more scaffolding before committing. I should push them gently to attempt writing T or V, even imperfectly — making an attempt is how learning happens. I'll ask them specifically about V first since it's simpler for a pendulum, and they can use their already-derived geometry. I should not give a template or the form of T and V.

exchanges.student: I think for one pendulum it’s something like \(V = MgL(1-\cos\theta)\), and for small angle that becomes \(\tfrac12 MgL\,\theta^2\). I’m still not seeing how to extend that cleanly to the second mass without getting lost in a bunch of terms, so I might need the more complete setup soon.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right for M1. Now, given your expression for X2 earlier, what is the *height* of M2 measured from its lowest point? Once you have that, writing V for M2 follows the same logic you just used.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified V for a single pendulum and applied the small-angle approximation. Good progress. Now I should ask them to extend this to M2 — they have all the tools: they know the height of M2 depends on both angles. I should not give the complete setup. I'll ask one focused question about the height of M2 to keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: I think for \(M_2\) the vertical position is something like \(y_2=-L\cos\theta_1-L\cos\theta_2\), so relative to the lowest point maybe the height is \(L(1-\cos\theta_1)+L(1-\cos\theta_2)\). Then \(V_2\) would be \(M_2 gL[(1-\cos\theta_1)+(1-\cos\theta_2)]\), but I’m not confident about the reference point or whether that’s the form I actually want before small-angle expanding.
exchanges.tutor: That's correct — the reference point choice doesn't affect the equations of motion, so your expression is fine. Now write down the total V = V1 + V2 and apply the small-angle approximation (which you already know how to do). What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified V2. This is good progress. I should affirm this briefly and push them to now write the total V and apply the small-angle approximation — they already know how to do the latter. This moves us forward without giving anything away.

exchanges.student:
I get
\[
V = M_1 gL(1-\cos\theta_1)+M_2 gL\big[(1-\cos\theta_1)+(1-\cos\theta_2)\big].
\]
So for small angles I think that becomes
\[
V \approx \tfrac12 gL\big[(M_1+M_2)\theta_1^2 + M_2\theta_2^2\big].
\]
I’m not sure if it’s a bad sign that there’s no \(\theta_1\theta_2\) coupling in \(V\); that makes me feel like I’m missing where the actual coupled-oscillator part enters.
exchanges.tutor: Your V is correct, and your instinct is sharp. So ask yourself: where *else* could coupling enter? Think about the kinetic energy T of M2. What is the velocity of M2 in terms of θ1 and θ2 and their time derivatives?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived V and is showing good physical intuition by noticing the absence of cross-coupling in V. This is actually correct and worth affirming. The coupling comes from T, not V, in this problem. I should ask them to now think about T for M2, which is where the coupling lives — without telling them that directly. I'll ask them to write the velocity of M2.

exchanges.student: That’s the part where I start getting tangled. I think this is one of those “different angle” moments again: I can tell the coupling probably shows up in \(T\), but when I try differentiating the coordinates I lose track of what to keep in the small-angle limit. Could you help me set up \(v_2^2\) cleanly, maybe in the most general form first?
exchanges.tutor: You already have X2 = L sinθ1 + L sinθ2. You also wrote y2 = -L cosθ1 - L cosθ2 earlier. Just differentiate both with respect to time — don't apply small-angle yet. What do you get for Ẋ2 and ẏ2?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to set up v2^2 for them, which I should not do. However, they already have X2 = L sinθ1 + L sinθ2 from earlier, so I should point them back to their own work and ask them to differentiate it. I'll also remind them that v2^2 = (Ẋ2)^2 + (ẏ2)^2 and let them take it from there. Keeping it minimal.

exchanges.student:
I think
\[
\dot X_2 = L\cos\theta_1\,\dot\theta_1 + L\cos\theta_2\,\dot\theta_2,
\]
and
\[
\dot y_2 = L\sin\theta_1\,\dot\theta_1 + L\sin\theta_2\,\dot\theta_2.
\]
Then I’m guessing \(v_2^2=\dot X_2^2+\dot y_2^2\), but I’m worried the algebra is about to explode and I’ll miss the point.
exchanges.tutor: Good, those are correct. Now square and add them. As you expand, look carefully at the coefficients of the cross terms and the squared terms — do you notice any familiar trig identities that simplify things?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly differentiated the coordinates. Now they need to square and add these to get v2^2. The algebra does look intimidating but simplifies nicely using sin^2+cos^2=1 and the small-angle approximation. I should ask them to expand v2^2 and look for a simplification — nudging them toward the trig identity without giving it away.

exchanges.student:
I think it turns into
\[
v_2^2=L^2\dot\theta_1^2+L^2\dot\theta_2^2+2L^2\dot\theta_1\dot\theta_2(\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2+\sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2),
\]
so maybe
\[
v_2^2=L^2\left(\dot\theta_1^2+\dot\theta_2^2+2\dot\theta_1\dot\theta_2\cos(\theta_1-\theta_2)\right).
\]
If that’s right, I’m still hazy on what I’m allowed to simplify next in the small-angle limit, because I don’t want to drop the wrong coupling term.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — well done. Now, for small angles, what does cos(θ1 - θ2) simplify to? And importantly, will that term still carry coupling information after the approximation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived v2^2 using the cosine subtraction identity — this is excellent progress. Now they just need to apply the small-angle approximation to cos(θ1-θ2). I should ask them directly what cos(θ1-θ2) becomes for small angles, which is a simple and direct step they can do themselves.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently guiding the student through the double pendulum problem via targeted questions rather than providing answers. Each turn built incrementally on the student's prior responses, and the tutor never gave away the final result or did the reasoning for the student. Responses were concise, well-targeted, and supportive in tone.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided near-submission-ready output, never rewrote student work, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Every turn posed a focused question that required the student to do the next reasoning step. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student progress (e.g., 'That's exactly right,' 'Good, those are correct') and built each subsequent question on what the student had just produced, with no restarts or ignored correct steps. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level guidance throughout, e.g., pointing out where coupling enters (T vs. V), reminding the student to look for trig identities, and flagging the small-angle approximation strategy — all method-level feedback. 2.1: No repetitive cycling; the conversation advanced steadily from degrees of freedom → geometry → potential energy → kinetic energy. 2.2: The student stayed on-task and the tutor kept the conversation anchored to Problem 3.1 throughout. 3.1: Responses were short and focused — typically one or two sentences plus a single question — appropriate for the interactive tutoring context. 3.2: Tone was consistently encouraging and supportive without being evaluative or grade-like.
